That's awesome. This is normally when I say, "Thanks mom," but
there was more than one person clapping, so that's good.
I've presented thousands of times to audiences, but typically, it's
an audience of two, or three, or maybe five. I think I'm going to
share some of those experiences with you today, but it's
important for me to first cover how we got here.
Good idea on the water, Mike. I love that.
My experience...and I'm not that much different from you:
average intelligence, medium height, good enough looking to get
enough dates along the way, during the course of my life, get a
couple good jobs - not that much different, had some success
along the way.
I think what is different about my experience that over the last 10
years I've had a different kind of selling, which is called pitching.
In selling you get to build a relationship with a customer, and
maybe go visit them a couple times and have some back and
forth and show them products.
In my experience I just got to walk into board rooms and pitch one
presentation, one bite of the apple one time to a group of one,
two, or three people. Boom, one shot, one kill.
When you put yourself in those situations where you don't have
many opportunities to come back and fix your mistakes you think
about every word; and you think about every nuance; you think
about every minute - what needs to happen; and you think about
what's happening and time slows down and everything becomes
relevant.
You can fly a thousand miles and spend $25,000 to go to one
meeting and have 20 minutes, and at minute 21 it's all over. Or
you might be in that room for an hour, but, as I always say, your
pitch is 20 minutes long and it's all the time you get.
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	1
Oren Klaff:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 20 --

So very high stakes pitches, very high pressure circumstance...my
partner, to be specific about how this all came together, so my
partner was very reckless, very wealthy guy, buy very reckless. He
would put our money $1 million or $2 million to lock up a deal. And
he's say, "Oren, you have 35 days to raise $5 million. You have 42
days to raise $7 million. You have 45 days to raise $20 million. And
that's it...you don't do it, and we lose our money."
So I lived that life for seven years and that's what gave birth to
these experiences. Large deals, high stakes, high pressure
consequences.
What I learned is, you go into a meeting and, just as it says here,
whether it's a phone call or you walk through the door or even on
the Internet, the immediately assess who has the power and who
is the most needy. It's all power. You know this in your heart.
It's funny, in some ways I might not be perfect for this audience
because I'm not motivational, as you can tell. I don't care what's
in your heart. I hope it's good, but I don't care.
I don't care if you're motivated. I don't care if you're sad right
now. My job, by the end of this 45 minutes is not to make you
happy. I'm not even going to try. You're going to laugh because
I'm a funny guy, but I'm not going to motivate you, I'm not going
to make you happy, I'm not going to make you sad. I don't care
about all those things.
The world that I function in has just three emotions: Angry,
hungry, and horny. That's it. That's what I have to deal with so I
can't think about who's happy and who's not. We all use that
expression... we're in a eat what you kill shop.
And eat what you kill shop...if you go to Wall Street, or some of
your family says, "I'm going to Wall Street," the first thing they say
when they walk in the door is, "You're in an eat what you kill
shop." That means you make nothing unless you close; unless
your pitch to close ratio is high. We are all descendants of humans
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	2
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 20 --

that just have these emotions: Angry, hungry, and horny. We're
going to talk a little bit about that.
In my world, what I've discovered is it is exhausting to absorb
factual information. And you know that from anytime you've had
to take a course or a seminar or get some training on a particular
subject: Calculus, algebra, accounting - it's very hard and at the
end of 45 minutes, you're exhausted. It's hard for the mind to
take in factual information, but you enjoy these social seminars,
you could do this all day. You can play sports even longer or do
things that are fun with your family.
Taking in factual information is exhausting so if you walk into a
room and the basic response is angry, hungry, and horny, and you
start giving people factual information then you have a real
problem on your hands. I had this problem for many years...walk
into a room, not knowing I had 20 minutes, giving people lots of
factual information over a lot of time, and my pitch is not
working. I'm not selling. I'm not closing deals. Eventually I learned
to fix this.
Let's go a little bit deeper into the problem. For those of you that
are familiar with my material, what I discovered is that these
selling environments, these pitching environments are all social.
So you walk into a room and you're interacting immediately with
the most primitive part of the human mind, the lizard brain, the
crocodile brain. Primitive - that's what you get to deal with first.
Then there's the mid-brain, which is the social brain. Finally, the
part of the brain that you want to talk to is the neocortex - that
you want to communicate, that can make a decision - that's the
last part of the mind you get access to.
This fundamental realization helped me close $500 million of
business. As I've said many times before, I'm not a biologist, I
barely got through high school biology. But if you don't know this
stuff, it's destructive to what you're trying to accomplish.
Let's run through it quickly, and you can sense this in your own
mind, and your body, and I'll explain how now. If you think about
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	3
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 20 --

it, you're walking through your own backyard...if you don't have
one because you live in the middle of Los Angeles or
Manhattan...or somebody else's backyard and you're rustling
around for a loose ball, and you see a snake. Boom, your nervous
system goes ballistic. Fear, sweat, you imagine all the horrible
things that are going to happen.
Your nervous system takes over - that's the croc-brain going,
"I'm afraid. Fear." Then, slowly, you recognize that you're in a
neighborhood and an ambulance can come get you if it was
necessary. Then your social brain kicks in and says, "Yes, this is a
bad situation, but at least I'm around friends and family and
there's ways for me to get saved." Then your neocortex kicks in
and starts aligning sight and all kinds of computational things
happen. And then you go, "Ah crap. That's not a snake, it's a
stick."
It might take five seconds, it might take three seconds to realize
it's a stick, but what happens is you are shaking for the next five,
six, seven, 10 minutes. The crocodile brain owns your nervous
system. Owns it.
Let's go back in the boardroom. You make somebody afraid of you
and the crocodile brain responds because it's the first responder
because all humans care about is survival. We don't care about
this stuff: Being in a conference. This was all learned and is a part
of our society, but being at a conference and learning how to do
the things that Paul teaches us how to do is amazing. By the way,
never follow that guy up on stage. That guy is freaking awesome.
You cannot possibly present as well as that guy.
This stuff is not what we do. We survive, that's what we we're built
to do. We dropped out of the trees into the African savanna and
ran eight miles a day looking for something to eat, something to
kill, and someone to mate with. That's what we're made to do.
Not this stuff. This stuff is a higher social order.
We're creatures of survival so when you walk into a board room,
the person you're presenting to is not going to listen to your
information, your linguistics, your math, and all those incredible
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	4
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 20 --

things that you have to say. They're hypervigilant to fear. How can
this possibly damage me? They're focused on survival, so your
idea has got to come into that crocodile brain, and then it's got to
get into the social brain, and then it gets into the neocortex where
they can absorb some information, which is exhausting, and try
and make some decisions about what you have.
This is why I said the crucial moment, when it's most important to
be convincing, nine of 10 times we are not, or 10 out of 10 times
for some people, because we have to understand how
information flows through the other person. Let's go deeper into
this subject now.
Let's talk about the selling environment. It used to be that you
could offer some value to someone, something valuable. You
could package it up and take it in the market, and. . . . Who spelled
customers there? Damn it. That was me. So, you take it in the
market, and you offer it to people, and you get some customers,
and it kind of works. Now, today, there's a huge amount of what I
call, "white noise." The signal-to-noise ratio of what you have is
low.
I want to give you a quick example. I used to work for a company
called British Technology Group as a technology analyst. They had
me going to Jet Propulsion Labs in Pasadena, and Caltech, and
Lawrence Livermore National Labs, and they said, "Go find great
technologies at the patent level. Go find scientists and bring us.
You're a hunter and gatherer for technology. Go bring us
technology."
I remember one day I go to Jet Propulsion Labs, and they take me
19 floors down in the basement, and they introduce me to a
Russian mad scientist. I said, "Tell me about your technology," and
he says, "I have a low light camera that sees into space one
kabillion hundred five thousand millions miles further than the
next guy." "That's amazing, show me this thing." It's have the size
of this rooms. It's lenses, and it's semiconductors, and it goes
down to the one micron, and there's a specially developed
blackout paint, and it's amazing, this technology.
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	5
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 20 --

I said, "My God, you must be so proud of yourself, but isn't it
lonely? Isn't it lonely? You developed all this amazing technology
that can see into space further than anyone else?" He says, "It's
not that bad. We have two conferences a year: One in Slovenia
and one in London." I said, "Who has this conference?" "Oh, all of
the guys with low light cameras that can see billions of miles into
space."
Nothing you are doing is that unique. In these most esoteric,
unbelievably sophisticated technologies that take mad scientists
their whole careers to develop, and there are 40 other guys that
do it, and they get together twice a year to drink beer. They sell
protein powder on the Internet, and there's now 20, 40, 1,000
other guys doing it.
There's a huge amount of noise. There are many, many people
clamoring for the same space, so what do I see happening?
Especially on the Internet, but certainly in pitching and selling,
"Well, if there's lots of white noise, then we turn up the signal."
Probably half of the people in this room, including myself, have
done that. Writing in all caps. Writing "AMAZING" and "EXCELLENT"
in all caps. What is that? That's turning up the signal, the volume,
because you're trying to break through.
I've got this great product, but nobody will listen to me, so I've got
to turn up the volume. All caps. Exclamation points. "Oh, let's
highlight it in yellow. Let's circle it in red." Turn up the volume,
amplify everything. Now, we do break through. This signal-to-
noise ratio goes higher, we break through the white noise when
we turn up the volume, but what happens also? We get people's
attention, but we start to create fear, because we've got a signal
that's so much higher than people are expecting to see.
When you sell so aggressively, you do get people's attention, and
you do break through the white noise, but also, in the crocodile
brain, you trigger fear. I'll just go back to that, because this is
where everything comes up through. When we raise the volume
so aggressively, we trigger fear. Then what happens is people go
to conversion funnels. We do it ourselves, and you have to do it,
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	6
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 20 --

but conversion funnel goes more into analytics, and it goes
further away from the social environment.
Here's my conclusion: When you're selling something, or you're
pitching a deal, you don't have an option for this. Your ideas, your
concepts, the things you want to say come up through the
crocodile brain. You have to just do that in a way that's safe. You
can't screw that up, so you've got to watch how much volume
you're applying to not create fear.
Well, you can't deliver information right away, because
information is exhausting to consume and you burn people out.
The minute you start delivering information, the clock starts
ticking, and you may not have enough time. To me, it's all social.
It all happens in this midbrain area. This midbrain developed,
because as we move from individuals seeking to eat something,
kill something, mate from something, we moved into tribes, and
we moved into communities, and this part of the brain developed
to track large communities within our own tribe and other tribes,
and it's very, very powerful.
I'll just one thing quickly, think about a three year-old doesn't
know algebra, doesn't know calculus, doesn't know Shakespeare.
Maybe some of the people in this people are damn smart and
their three year-olds can do all of that stuff, but the three year-
olds in my family just can't do that. They barely walk. But a three
year-old knows very, very broad social environments. They know
who's a policeman, they know who's a teacher, they know who's
an aunt, an uncle, who's a brother, who's a mom, and who can
deliver the power stroke, and who they can control.
They don't know anything reading, and Shakespeare, and
linguistics, and math, but they have a very, very broad
understanding of social environments. You can see it happening.
You see a stick that you think is a snake, it takes over your
nervous system. You see a three year-old, they understand the
difference between a cop, and a teacher, and a mom, and a
neighbor, and somebody they've never met. Very, very broad
social environments. Finally, you can know in your own mind
when you have to listen to information, it is very exhausting.
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	7
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 20 --

I believe it all boils down to, what can you do in the social
environment? I think today, the problem is, we deliver information
to someone, and then they begin to reject us. Maybe some
people, a small percentage, say, "I love it, I want to buy," we
deliver a value proposition, and then they begin to reject us. This
is why I say selling today, or pitching, is based on rejection
control. Rejection control, which is, "Oh, you are objecting to my
sale. Let me overcome your objection." Well, we don't do that at
all.
Oh, I can definitely hear you guys back there. Okay. Note to self:
Mic.
Overcoming objections has become the thing that we do. We give
some information, "Buy my product. Invest in my deal. Do what I
say," and then we get an objection back, and then we try to
overcome that objection. It's rejection control.
I watch most of my contemporaries try and sell something, and
what happens is, they deliver information to the buyer, then the
buyer asks a question, then they answer the question, and then
the buyer asks another question, and then they answer it, and it's
like a game of Gnip Gnop, or tennis. I just see it going back and
forth. There's no social control in there, nothing is happening, it's
just information moving back and forth. It's not good.
Rejection control, unlike traditional sales methods, are
predictable, and they're easy to defeat. They take no training.
In my experience, you walk into a room, you start to deliver
information to somebody to buy your deal: "We're selling a
company. It's $15,000,000. We'd like you to pay $15,000,000 for
it. Here's the information." "Well, how do you guys account for the
fact that there's not good transparency and visibility in the next
three quarters of revenue?" "Oh, let me tell you how we account
for that." "Well, what about the product? Aren't there
competitors?" "Oh, here's a competitive landscape." "Well, the
management team, is the management team any good? I'm
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	8
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 20 --

looking at their bios, it doesn't look like they've done this before."
"Oh, let me tell you about the management team."
That's all rejection control, so what I've learned is it doesn't work
very well. Now, let's move forward to what I think does work.
Let me say one more thing: Economic transactions are about
power. You walk into a room, and the guy with the money has the
power. We immediately assess, as I've said before, "Who's the
purchasing power? Who has the information power? Who has the
staying power to last longest?" These aren't my terms, you guys
are all familiar with it. "Oh, they have good purchasing power."
"Who has staying power? Who has the most options?" The guy
with the most options has the most power, and that's always the
money, right?
You walk into a room, and you immediately bump into this power
frame. "Well, we have the money, so you, pitch me your product.
Let me listen to you. I will be the judge of what you have. And I'll
be the judge of what you have." Sell me something. Because I
have money and I have the power, like the Star Wars Senator
Palpatine. Power, right?
Who has the power? You walk in a room, the guy with the money
has the power and that's a real problem because it's not fun to
play power games so when somebody is the king and they can
judge you, they have the power. And you're sitting there so that
makes you the supplicant.
Supplicant, as I've said before, is a person who's trying to prove
that they're worth the purchase order or getting the money or
getting approval. But that's the buyer-seller relationship. That's
the buyer-seller relationship. The other guy has the money, he
has the power and we are trying to sell a product so we have to
supplicate to him.
The power frame always tries to get economic concessions from
you in exchange for intangibles. That's what power does, right? So
the bigger the boardroom.
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	9
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 20 --

I had a pitch two months ago at 2 Embarcadero. You think this is
nerve wrecking sitting in front of 500 people. I mean, it's only 10
people. Two Embarcadero overlooking the entire San Francisco
Bay. A conference room table that goes from here to the back of
the room so a hockey rink. Artwork on the wall worth more than
I've made in my entire life, right? Billionaires sitting there. It's
power, right?
And you get a sense like I want to give a concession to these guys
just to be on their playing field. Think of everything they can do for
me. If I'm in business with them, they can introduce me to other
guys I want to know. I'm going to give them a good price, because
of all the power they have. And they use it, right? They want to get
economic concessions. They want me to lower my price and give
me nothing.
And I do it. Because it's the power frame. So let's talk about ways
to deal with this.
Back to the brain. Whenever then we encounter the power frame,
we start to sell hard. We get a sense of we're going to have to give
an economic concession. Let me give like a little bit of effort
before I concede. We start to sell hard. Then what happens? The
primordial response of their crock brain kicks in and they become
afraid of us and their power goes up. Our status goes lower and
we have a harder time. We created fear. We're not in a social
environment and we're nowhere near being able to pitch the
neocortex. It's a horrible situation.
The crock brain is a gate keeper. We got to get past it.
So let's, I think, that makes the problem deeply felt. You walk into
a room. The other guys have the power. We start to supplicate,
they start to use their power so we start to sell harder. There's lots
of white noise. We feel like our signal to noise ratio is low and we
start to pump up the volume on our messaging. Pump up the
volume of the messaging, the defensive mechanisms of the crock
brain kick in. We're not working on a social environment, which
the mind responds to and now we have all kinds of selling and
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	10
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 20 --

pitching and presenting problems. And things start going
cattywampus. Start going cattywampus is not good.
So, if you look at this, we start to say, "I need to solve this," and
for me, if you believe you have to take care of the crock brain and
you can't do much better to organize your information then it's all
social, so what tools do we have to work in a social environment?
This is the last time we'll visit the brain, right? So you have to take,
when I say, take care of the crock brain I mean something very
specific. You go in. You get somebody on the phone. You walk into
a room. You get them on the Internet site. You have to take care
of the crock brain so it doesn't runaway from you. Then you have
to do things fast. You can't wax theoretically. You have to convey
information that's meaningful and do it fast to be novel. Nobody
comes to a damn meeting to hear about things that they already
know from people they already know, about outcomes they can
guess.
They only come to a meeting or conference or fly thousands of
miles and pay money to do something because they're going to
learn something new and see colorful people and hear about
interesting things and go to exotic places and experience new
experiences. That's the only reason people will come to a phone
call or a meeting or an Internet page. For novelty. You have to
provide it.
And has to be concrete. The second you're vague and fuzzy, this
crocodile mind goes I'm not afraid of it, it's boring and it's too
complicated. I'm out of here and do some things related to
survival. This doesn't affect me. So it's got to be concrete. It has to
be visual. The pipes into the human mind from the eyes go 50 to a
100 times faster than auditory or sensory.
So if you really want access to the mind you go visual. Imagine
driving to work at one mile an hour. You'll get there very fast. Just
for comparisons sake. It's got to be visual and it's got to be high
contrast. This crock brain doesn't understand things that are
similar to each other. Fast, novel, concrete, visual, high contrast.
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	11
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 20 --

So now we enter what I believe. You walk into a room, you enter
the power frame and if there's such a thing as a power frame,
then what do you want? You want frame control. This is what I've
said many times and hopefully this is maybe the second or third
or fourth time you're hearing it.
But let me tell you what my definition of a frame is. Definition of a
frame. It's interesting, when I was writing the book I thought it
was going to be easy to define frames. I bought fifty, sixty books
from psychologists at Columbia and MIT and Harvard, Yale. All
about frames and frame control and none of them had good
definitions that were usable. So here's my definition.
Each of us has a frame. We come to a meeting and we bring a
frame with us. That's our point. That's our perspective. It's our
charisma. It's all the information we have. So I think my deal is
worth $1 million. I'm sorry, I think my deal is worth $5 million. And
you bring your frame and you think the deal is worth $1 million
and these frames come together in a meeting. You can watch
them in slow motion.
$5 million and $1 million are incompatible points of view. If a
frame is a point of view then these frames collide and they crash
into each other and they bend and crumble like a car accident and
they smash each other and eventually one frame is always the
stronger frame and it crushes and breaks the weaker frame and it
absorbs it and you know this. Because in any meeting you've ever
been in where there is an outcome there is one stronger
perspective, one stronger point of view, one stronger frame and
that frame dominates.
So we're always seeking frame control where our point of view is
the dominant point of view. Now it doesn't mean we're alpha
males, even if we're females, exhibiting alpha behavior,
aggressive, and dominant and it's not what we're looking for.
We're looking for a dominant frame and we can do it the most
relaxed and poised and simple and easy and calm way. We don't
have to be at battle stations to get frame control but what we do
need is some tools to get it. It's not about elevating voice and
aggression and being the alpha animal.
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	12
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 20 --

So, in a social environment we avoid crock brain problems and
then we look for tools to get frame control. And some of those
tools are to elevate your status because people fall into the frame
of people who have high status. Status comes from celebrity,
influence and money. Status comes so when you have a celebrity
Brad Pitt or Morgan Freeman, you defer to him. He wants to go to
this restaurant, you want to go somewhere else, you go to where
he wants to go. He's a celebrity.
The commander of the armed forces wants to go to a restaurant,
you go with him. He's got influence. You're not going to go against
him. And Donald Trump wants to go to a restaurant, he's got very
high status because he has money. So status comes from money,
comes from influence and comes with celebrity. You want to
create some status for yourself.
You can control these power frames with time frames. Time
frames control outcomes. So you guys all know this. If you go to
me and you say, "You have five minutes." You know that meeting
is all about the delivery of information because there's not going
to be any social relationships but if you say it's a two hour
meeting, then there's going to be all kinds of social interactions.
So time frames are a way of controlling power.
We'll talk about how to do this specifically.
Let's talk about prizing. So this is something that I developed to
overcome the power frame. What I realize is, the power frame is
something built on money. Now we're going to do some practical
tools. The power frame, you walk in a room, they have the money,
you don't. You're asking for a purchase order. You're asking for
them to write you a check. They're asking to buy what you have,
right? And you find yourself up in this supplicant. So when they
say, "You know, we like what you have, but why don't you fetch
me a blue rock? Because I need a little but more information." You
go fetch a blue rock to supplicate to the power frame.
They say, "I love the blue rock. Go fetch me a green rock now."
And you go get it, some information. And they say, "I love this
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	13
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 20 --

green rock. I'm ready to buy, but now I need a purple rock." And
you run and you go to get a purple rock. We supplicate to the
power frame.
So how do we take the power frame away? And from me, now,
we're going to enter social tools of framing. And I thought about
this and I tried many, many things. And I lost many, many deals to
the point where my partners were getting frustrated with me.
"You don't know what you're doing. You don't know how to sell.
You don't know how to deliver information. You don't know how
to answer questions. You don't know how to overcome
objections."
But I believe that rejection control would work. I believe social
control, frame control would work. So eventually I came up with
this concept of prizing. Where you just take the money away.
Okay mister buyer, you're not the judge of me. Because all you
have is money and I don't care about money. Money is a
commodity. I can get that from an ATM machine. I can go get a
loan. I can spend less. Money is a commodity. It's more of a
commodity than rice. It's more of a commodity than oil.
Money is a commodity. You do not get to make the decisions. You
do not get to tell me to go fetch another rock. You don't get to
control this agenda because you have money, because I don't
care about money. Money is a commodity and it's available
anywhere. Nobody has what I have. The blue plastic frogs that I
have are the baddest in the world. They kill insects deader over
more coverage, faster. And they're cute. Look here, you bastard,
I'm in the business of having them, and you're in the business of
wanting them. So there's some confusion in this room and
nobody's fetching a rock. Right?
And the practical you do this is say, "Money, we don't care about.
There's only one of me. I'm the best blue frog plastic salesman in
the world. I've been in France. I've studied frogs for ten years. I
know. I've seen insects die, on film, a slow death. There is nobody
in the world that is more integral, harder working, knows more
about these things, cares more about you as a customer. And
nobody carries a better product than this. I'm the prize." There's
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	14
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 20 --

some basic confusion in this room about where the power is. So
let me explain it to you.
Now you're not going to say it in these terms. If there's anything
motivational in here, it's this, you've got to internalize, what you
have is the prize. And the other guy only has money. All right? And
you're not going to make an economic concession from money,
because you can get it anywhere.
And let me tell you how you do this, both inside yourself and
outwardly. I'm the best in the world at this. And it might be a
small business. Blue plastic frogs might not be on the news every
night. But it's important because you've got to kill insects,
otherwise they eat crops and you can't sell your products. You
can't take your corn to market unless you have these things. I'm
the best in the world at that. All right?
The issue is, I can only take on ten customers a month. And I have
eight or nine right now. So I'm looking for one additional
customer. So why don't you tell me why I should let you be my
customer. I just want to talk about the right time to do this.
So, many times I will talk to people about this and then they will
rush off. And I'll get a call three days later, or an email. "I hate you.
You've ruined my business." What happened? What did you do?
"Well just like you said. I walked in a room and I said why should I
let you be my customer? I'm the best in the world and all you have
is money. So I'll leave. I'm going to call myself a cab unless you tell
me why I'll let you be my customer."
So, we have to think about how to build up to the point where we
can say this. Let's try and do that now. So first of all, we're not
going to spend too much time on this but one minute, because
this is maybe an eight-hour conversation, is the attention
problem.
We have to know how to hold people's attention, not scare them.
And not give too much detail. And not make what we have to
vague or fuzzy. And make it seem too similar to what they've seen
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	15
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 20 --

before. Or go to slow. Or worst of all, seem needy. You can't have
these problems happening. So that's one step in the process.
But along the way, we're going to use all these frames, all these
social tools that we have, the time frame. So you want to know
how to prize yourself? It's very simple. I think I've said in most of
the videos, and I'll say it every time I talk to someone. I have
about 20 minutes to pitch you what I have.
And then, I'm interested to hear from you in context of what I
have, what you're doing and why you would be a good customer.
Why you would be a good investor. Why I'd want to work with you.
And because I don't care that much about money, but I care
mostly about having fun, I would be fun to do things with you. And
we could have a relationship. And how, if something goes wrong,
you won't act like a jerk. And how you've behaved in investor
relationships before. And how you've behaved in buyer-seller
relationships before. And when you bought your last copier, I'm
very interested to know how you treated the copier salesman.
So I'm going to pitch for 20 minutes, I have about an hour for Q&A
after that, and then I have to go. So we can use time frames. We
can understand the power frame when we see it. We can get
control of the needy frame so we're never needy. We can talk
more about that in a minute.
When we see the analyst frame coming at us, asking us questions,
boom, boom, boom. What are the manufacturing specifications?
How do I know it'll last in the field as long as you say it will? How
do I know that somebody else doesn't have a better price?
You can get control of that frame in a very social way and say,
"Those are great questions. We have a binder here with all the
answers in it. But let's not waste the time that we have together
as people. Let's find out if we even like each other, if we even
want to do business with each other. And then we can get into
details of the binder. The analysts can get offline and look at the
binder with the specifications in it. Let's work together as
individuals and find out if we even like each other, if we're going
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	16
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 20 --

to do business together. If it even makes sense to try and get to a
deal."
So that's how we're going to overcome the analyst frame, with
very social tools. We use the intrigue frame to counteract the
power frame. Moral authority frame is very interesting. The moral
authority frame, if you think about it, when the power frame is
saying to you, "I want you to make a price concession." And that
price concession is painful to you. And you say, "Hey John, I will let
you do anything. You can negotiate hard. You can grind me. You
can ask for better delivery times. But the price you're asking for, I
won't let you ask me to be a bad business person. That's one thing
I won't allow you to do." So you can use the moral authority
frame.
As we talked here before, the money frame, you just take away
the value of money. Say, "Hey, I have all the money I need. And we
have tons of customers. So what's important to me is having fun
and liking people that I work with. So forget about the money,
why don't you tell me how it's fun to work with you." So you can
use the money frame.
The value frame is very important. When you're getting ground on,
whether it's on the Internet by e-mail, or in person, like I have in
conference rooms. So the other side is asking you tons of
questions, making you on the defensive, and you're reacting to
people you can use the value frame.
So let me say one thing before we cover the value frame. A lot of
people ask me about frame control. How do you know when you
have frame control? When you are reacting to the other person,
they have frame control. All right. When they are reacting to you,
you have frame control. That's simply how you know.
So when somebody's asking you tons of questions and making
you prove yourself, and qualify yourself, they have frame control.
And you get it back with saying, "Hey look, we're going to get past
all these questions, because I wouldn't be here, selling you this
thing if it wasn't great. I wouldn't be selling you this deal if it
wasn't great."
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	17
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 20 --

So beyond this meaning are you able to add any value to this
relationship. Because we're looking for value, not questions.
When you ask people what their value is, then the frame will shift
as they're qualifying themselves to you. So these are all social
tools.
So now we're going to talk about the prize frame and using it, so
we can be real specific. Here's three basic tools for using the prize
frame.
One, we're going to walk in the room. We say, "Hey." When I walk
in the room, what always happens is people will say, "Let us tell
you about our company. Let us tell you about our business."
And I say, "Stop. Don't tell me about that." As I've said in the video
and in the book, I'm telling you here. Stop people from telling you
about themselves. Say, "I'll tell you what I have and then you
reflect back to me. You reflect back to me later after I've told you
what I have, about yourselves. I want to hear about yourselves in
context of what I have. In about 20 minutes, I'm going to pitch to
you what I have, and then we can do about an hour of Q&A."
Then you go into your pitch. And that's the book and it's beyond
the scope of this conversation here, but the strong method which
is: set the frame, tell the story, reveal the intrigue, offer the prize,
nail the hook point, get the deal.
You use the strong method in your pitch to really move human
emotion back and forth. Use all these frames. Use all these frames
within your pitch to build your status and your own celebrity in
that circumstance.
You're going to give this great pitch and you're going to say, we're
very busy. We have lots of customers. We're doing great. This
product is great. I'm the best in the world at it. I understand these
blue plastic frogs market.
Oil, the price of oil is going up. The technology for manufacturing
frogs is getting better and better, right? But there's huge demand
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	18
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 18 of 20 --

in France. So you have to understand these forces. When to buy
and how much. I'm a celebrity in this business. I know all this
stuff.
And you're going to pitch your product. Eventually you're going to
pitch it so well using that strong method and using these frames.
Time frames, moral authority frame, overcoming the analyst
frame, all these social methods.
And at some point, what's going to happen is your status, and
your celebrity, and the way you do things is so great that people
are going to be falling into your frame, and liking you and you
have momentum.
At that point, when you have the most amount of momentum,
and you're close to your 20 minute mark, you are now going to be
able to use the prize frame very effectively. You can apply counter
pressure.
When people like what you have, when they like you as a person,
when they want your product, when you're giving and amazing
pitch, and you're a celebrity because you understand the moving
forces within the market that they're looking at and you can really
help them.
You're providing insight. You have this momentum, and star
power, and status, and you're controlling the frames, and people
are liking you and want what you have. Then you're at a hook
point because you feel them leaning in. The energy moves into
you. You feel them in your frame.
At the point that you have maximum status, momentum, then
you're going to say, "I'm the prize. I want to hear from you why
you would be a good customer, or why you would be a good
investor, and why it'd be a good idea for me to work with you."
Then, that's when you get social control. If you do this correctly,
they will qualify themselves back to you. They will fall into to the
prize frame. It's amazing when you experience it. They will say,
"Oh, I'll be a good customer. I promise you."
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	19
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 20 --

Now, I say this in the video, if you do this really well... I'm good at
this. I'm good at this. I do it really well. When you do this really
well, as I hope all of you accomplish, you'll see some crazy things
start to happen.
They'll spill their coffee. As I've said before, they'll try and tell
stories the way you tell them, but they'll mess them up, right?
They'll just do weird things trying to be you, but they can't be you
because you're the prize. Friggin' awesome. So when you see
people falling apart, you know you have prized with maximum
effect.
And I want to see everybody here doing that. That's the time I
have here today. I think that is something you can leave here and
start doing. And we hope to talk to you and meet you all
somewhere along the road.
Keynote
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	20
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 20 of 20 --
